Question: What are the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming? Explain each briefly.
Expert_Answer: (1) Encapsulation: binding data and methods, hiding internal details via access modifiers [1]. Getter/setter provide controlled access [1]. (2) Inheritance: new class inherits properties and methods of parent using 'extends' [1]. Promotes code reusability [1]. (3) Polymorphism: same method behaves differently [1]. Compile-time: method overloading [1]. Runtime: method overriding [1]. (4) Abstraction: hiding implementation, showing only relevant features via abstract classes and interfaces [1].
Keywords: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, extends, overloading, overriding, access modifiers
Misconceptions: Encapsulation is the same as abstraction | Polymorphism only refers to method overloading
Follow_up: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?

Question: Explain the difference between static and instance variables/methods in Java.
Expert_Answer: Static variables/methods belong to the class, shared by all objects, accessed via ClassName.method() [2]. Created when class loads, one copy in memory [2]. Static methods cannot access instance variables or use 'this' [2]. Static blocks execute when class loads [2]. Instance variables/methods belong to each object; each has its own copy [2]. Instance blocks execute before constructors [2]. Main method is static because JVM calls it without creating an object [2].
Keywords: static, instance, class variable, object, shared, this keyword, static block, instance block
Misconceptions: Static methods can access instance variables | Static variables are always thread-safe
Follow_up: When would you use a static factory method instead of a constructor?

Question: Explain types of constructors in Java: default, parameterized, and copy constructors.
Expert_Answer: Default constructor: no-argument [3]. Java provides one automatically only if no constructor is defined [3]. Parameterized constructor: accepts arguments to initialize fields with specific values; multiple parameterized constructors = constructor overloading [3]. Copy constructor: takes object of same class and copies its values (Java has no built-in copy constructor like C++) [3]. Constructor chaining: this() calls same class constructor; super() calls parent – both must be the first statement [3].
Keywords: default constructor, parameterized constructor, copy constructor, constructor overloading, this(), super(), constructor chaining
Misconceptions: Java always provides a default constructor | Constructors have a return type
Follow_up: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy?

Question: Explain inheritance types in Java. Why doesn't Java support multiple inheritance through classes?
Expert_Answer: Java supports: Single, Multilevel (A extends B extends C), Hierarchical (multiple classes extend one parent) [4]. Java does NOT support multiple inheritance through classes due to the Diamond Problem – ambiguity when two parent classes have the same method [4]. Java resolves this with interfaces (a class can implement multiple) [4]. 'final' class cannot be inherited [4]. Method hiding: static methods in child hide parent static (not overriding) [4].
Keywords: single inheritance, multilevel inheritance, hierarchical inheritance, diamond problem, interface, super, final
Misconceptions: Java supports multiple inheritance through classes | Method hiding is the same as overriding
Follow_up: Explain method hiding vs method overriding with an example.

Question: Explain runtime polymorphism (method overriding) and compile-time polymorphism (method overloading).
Expert_Answer: Method Overloading (compile-time): same method name, different parameter list in same class [5]. Resolved at compile time (static binding) [5]. Return type alone cannot differentiate [5]. Example: add(int,int) and add(double,double) [5]. Method Overriding (runtime): subclass redefines parent's method with same name and signature [5]. Resolved at runtime (dynamic binding) [5]. @Override recommended [5]. Cannot override static, private, or final methods [5]. Covariant return type is allowed [5].
Keywords: method overloading, method overriding, compile-time, runtime, dynamic binding, static binding, @Override, covariant return type
Misconceptions: Overloading and overriding are interchangeable | You can override static methods in Java
Follow_up: What is dynamic method dispatch? Give a code example.

Question: Differentiate between Abstract Classes and Interfaces in Java. When would you use each?
Expert_Answer: Abstract Class: can have abstract and concrete methods, constructors, instance variables [6]. Cannot be instantiated [6]. A class can extend only one [6]. Use when classes share common state and behavior [6]. Interface: all methods abstract by default (Java 8+: default and static methods allowed) [6]. No instance variables (only constants) [6]. A class can implement multiple interfaces [6]. Use for defining contracts [6]. Java 8 default methods allow code reuse without breaking existing implementations [6].
Keywords: abstract class, interface, abstract method, default method, implements, extends, multiple inheritance, contract
Misconceptions: Abstract classes and interfaces are interchangeable | Interfaces cannot have any concrete code
Follow_up: From Java 8, what additions to interfaces reduce the need for abstract classes?

Question: Explain Exception Handling in Java. Differentiate Checked and Unchecked exceptions.
Expert_Answer: Exceptions disrupt normal program flow [7]. Hierarchy: Throwable → Error (serious, unrecoverable) and Exception [7]. Checked exceptions: must be caught or declared (throws) [7]. Compile-time check [7]. Examples: IOException, SQLException [7]. Unchecked (RuntimeException): not required to be caught [7]. Examples: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException [7]. try-catch-finally: try has risky code, catch handles, finally always executes [7]. throw: explicitly throw [7]. throws: declare [7].
Keywords: exception, checked, unchecked, try-catch-finally, throw, throws, RuntimeException, IOException
Misconceptions: Finally block does not execute if an exception occurs | All exceptions should be caught
Follow_up: What is the difference between throw and throws? Give an example of each.

Question: How do you create a User-Defined Exception in Java? Give an example.
Expert_Answer: User-defined exceptions extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked) [8]. Example: class InsufficientFundsException extends Exception { public InsufficientFundsException(String msg) { super(msg); } } [8]. Usage: if(balance < amount) throw new InsufficientFundsException("Insufficient balance") [8]. Calling method: void withdraw(double amount) throws InsufficientFundsException [8]. Best practices: use checked for recoverable situations, unchecked for programming errors [8].
Keywords: user-defined exception, custom exception, extends Exception, throws, throw, checked, RuntimeException
Misconceptions: All custom exceptions must extend Exception directly | User-defined exceptions cannot have constructors
Follow_up: When should a custom exception be checked vs unchecked?

Question: Explain the Java Collections Framework. Compare ArrayList, Vector, LinkedList, HashMap, and HashSet.
Expert_Answer: ArrayList: dynamic array, O(1) access, O(n) insert/delete middle, not thread-safe [9]. Vector: like ArrayList but synchronized (thread-safe), slower [9]. LinkedList: doubly linked list, O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at known position, implements Deque [9]. HashMap: key-value, O(1) average, allows one null key, not thread-safe [9]. HashSet: unique elements backed by HashMap, O(1) operations [9]. TreeMap/TreeSet: sorted, O(log n) [9].
Keywords: ArrayList, Vector, LinkedList, HashMap, HashSet, Collections, thread-safe, Iterator
Misconceptions: HashMap allows duplicate keys | ArrayList is always faster than LinkedList
Follow_up: How would you synchronize an ArrayList? What are the alternatives?

Question: Explain the Thread Life Cycle in Java. How do you create threads?
Expert_Answer: Thread Life Cycle: New → Runnable → Running → Blocked/Waiting/Timed-Waiting → Terminated [10]. Two creation methods: (1) Extend Thread class, override run(), call start() [10]. (2) Implement Runnable interface, pass to Thread constructor – preferred because it allows extending other classes [10]. Key methods: start() – creates new thread and calls run(), sleep(ms), join() – wait for thread to finish, yield(), interrupt() [10]. Priority: 1-10, default 5 [10].
Keywords: thread lifecycle, Runnable, Thread class, start(), run(), sleep(), join(), thread priority
Misconceptions: Calling run() directly creates a new thread | Higher priority threads always execute first
Follow_up: What is the difference between wait() and sleep() in Java?

Question: Explain synchronization in Java. What are synchronized methods and synchronized blocks?
Expert_Answer: Synchronization prevents race conditions [11]. Synchronized method: only one thread executes on same object at a time using the intrinsic lock (monitor) [11]. Synchronized block: finer control – synchronized(this) { count++; } – can synchronize on any object [11]. Static synchronized: class-level lock [11]. Volatile: ensures visibility but not atomicity [11]. Java 5 java.util.concurrent: ReentrantLock, Semaphore, CountDownLatch – more flexible than synchronized [11].
Keywords: synchronized, monitor, intrinsic lock, volatile, ReentrantLock, race condition, thread safety
Misconceptions: Synchronized methods prevent all race conditions | Volatile ensures atomicity
Follow_up: What is a deadlock in Java multithreading and how do you avoid it?

Question: Explain Java I/O streams. Differentiate between Byte Streams and Character Streams.
Expert_Answer: Byte Streams: read/write raw bytes [12]. Base classes: InputStream, OutputStream [12]. Examples: FileInputStream, FileOutputStream [12]. Used for binary files (images, audio) [12]. Character Streams: read/write Unicode characters [12]. Base classes: Reader, Writer [12]. Examples: FileReader, FileWriter, BufferedReader [12]. BufferedReader: readLine() method [12]. For user input: Scanner (parsing), BufferedReader (speed) [12]. Object streams: serialize/deserialize objects [12].
Keywords: byte stream, character stream, InputStream, Reader, Writer, FileInputStream, BufferedReader, serialization
Misconceptions: Byte streams only handle small files | Scanner and BufferedReader are interchangeable
Follow_up: What is Java serialization? How do you make a class serializable?

Question: Explain the differences between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer in Java.
Expert_Answer: String: immutable – each modification creates a new object [13]. String pool caches literals [13]. '+' in loops is inefficient O(n²) [13]. StringBuilder: mutable, not thread-safe [13]. Efficient for single-thread manipulation [13]. Methods: append(), insert(), delete(), reverse() [13]. StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized) [13]. Slower than StringBuilder [13]. Use StringBuilder for single-threaded, StringBuffer for multi-threaded [13]. Performance: StringBuilder O(n) vs String concatenation in loop O(n²) [13].
Keywords: String, StringBuilder, StringBuffer, immutable, mutable, thread-safe, string pool, append()
Misconceptions: String is mutable in Java | StringBuffer is always preferred over StringBuilder
Follow_up: Why is String immutable in Java? What are the advantages?

Question: Explain the JVM architecture and how Java achieves platform independence.
Expert_Answer: Java source (.java) compiled to bytecode (.class) by javac [14]. Bytecode is intermediate – not machine code [14]. JVM interprets/JIT-compiles bytecode for the specific OS/hardware – Write Once, Run Anywhere [14]. JVM components: (1) Class Loader, (2) Bytecode Verifier, (3) Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT Compiler that optimizes hot code), (4) Runtime Data Areas – Method Area, Heap (objects), Stack (frames), PC Register [14]. GC automatically manages heap memory [14].
Keywords: JVM, bytecode, JIT compiler, class loader, heap, stack, garbage collection, platform independence
Misconceptions: JVM is the same on all platforms | Java source code is compiled to machine code
Follow_up: What is JIT compilation and how does it improve Java performance?

Question: Explain Generics in Java. What problem do they solve?
Expert_Answer: Generics allow defining classes, interfaces, and methods with type parameters [15]. Example: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>() [15]. Problem solved: without generics, collections store Object – requires explicit casting and can cause ClassCastException at runtime [15]. Generics provide compile-time type safety [15]. Bounded type: <T extends Number> [15]. Wildcards: <?> unbounded, <? extends T> upper, <? super T> lower [15]. Type erasure: generic info removed at compile time for backward compatibility [15].
Keywords: generics, type parameter, type safety, bounded type, wildcard, type erasure, ClassCastException
Misconceptions: Generics work at runtime | List<Object> is a supertype of List<String>
Follow_up: What is type erasure and how does it affect generic programming in Java?

Question: Explain JDBC connectivity in Java. What are the steps to connect to a database?
Expert_Answer: JDBC provides API to connect Java programs to relational databases [16]. Steps: (1) Load driver – Class.forName() (modern drivers auto-register), (2) Get connection – DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password), (3) Create statement – Statement or PreparedStatement for parameterized queries, (4) Execute – executeQuery() for SELECT, executeUpdate() for DML, (5) Process ResultSet, (6) Close resources using try-with-resources [16]. PreparedStatement prevents SQL injection and precompiles queries [16].
Keywords: JDBC, Connection, PreparedStatement, ResultSet, DriverManager, SQL injection, executeQuery, executeUpdate
Misconceptions: Statement is always preferred over PreparedStatement | JDBC connection stays open forever automatically
Follow_up: How does PreparedStatement prevent SQL injection attacks?

Question: Explain the access modifiers in Java: private, default, protected, and public.
Expert_Answer: Private: accessible only within same class [17]. Not inherited [17]. Default (no modifier): accessible within same package only [17]. Protected: accessible within same package AND subclasses even in different packages [17]. Public: accessible from anywhere [17]. Best practice: fields private, access via public getter/setter [17]. A class can be public or default at the top level [17]. Inner class can access all outer class members including private [17].
Keywords: private, protected, public, default, access modifier, encapsulation, package
Misconceptions: Protected means private outside the class | Default and public access are the same
Follow_up: Can a subclass in a different package access a protected method of its parent?

Question: Explain the 'this' and 'super' keywords in Java with examples.
Expert_Answer: 'this' refers to the current object [18]. Uses: (1) Distinguish instance variable from local variable with same name: this.name = name, (2) Call another constructor in same class: this(param) – must be first statement, (3) Pass current object as argument, (4) Return current object for method chaining [18]. 'super' refers to parent class [18]. Uses: (1) Call parent constructor: super(params) – first statement, (2) Access overridden parent method: super.method(), (3) Access hidden parent variable [18].
Keywords: this keyword, super keyword, constructor chaining, method overriding, instance variable, parent class
Misconceptions: super() and this() can both be used as first statement | 'this' can be used in static context
Follow_up: Can you use both this() and super() in the same constructor?

Question: What is Java Garbage Collection? How does the JVM manage memory?
Expert_Answer: GC automatically reclaims memory of objects no longer referenced [19]. JVM heap: Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces) and Old Generation [19]. New objects go to Eden, survive GC → Survivor, multiple survivals → Old Gen [19]. GC types: Minor GC (Young Gen), Full GC (Old Gen) [19]. Algorithms: Serial, Parallel, CMS, G1 (default Java 9+) [19]. WeakReference allows GC under certain conditions [19]. Memory leaks still possible when objects are referenced but unused [19].
Keywords: garbage collection, heap, Young Generation, Old Generation, Eden, G1 GC, finalize, WeakReference
Misconceptions: GC eliminates all memory leaks | finalize() is always called before object is deleted
Follow_up: What causes StackOverflowError and OutOfMemoryError in Java?
